questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
In a saturated solution of the sparingly soluble electrolyte AgIO3 (molecular mass=283), the equilibrium which sets in is AgIO3 s⇌Ag+ aq+IO3- aq.
If the solubility product constant Ksp of AgIO3 at a given temperature is 1.0×10-8, what is the mass of AgIO3 in 100 mL of its saturated solution?
questions.solution:
AgIO3⇌Ag+ aq+IO3- aqSS
So, Ksp=S2,

or S2=1.0×10-8,

or S=1.0×10-4 mol L-1

=1.0×10-4×283 g L-1,

=1.0×10-4×2831000g mL-1,

=1.0×10-4×283×1001000g100mL,

=28.3×10-4 g100 mL,

=2.83×10-3 g100 mL.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
In an aqueous solution, the ionization constants for carbonic acid are

K 1 = 4.2 × 1 0 - 7 and  K 2 = 4.8 × 1 0 - 11

Select the correct statement for a saturated 0.034 M solution of the carbonic acid:
options: The concentration of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mtext>H</mtext> <mo>+</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math> is double that of <math> <mrow> <msubsup> <mtext>CO</mtext> <mn>3</mn> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> <mo>-</mo> </mrow> </msubsup> </mrow> </math>.

The concentration of <math> <mrow> <msubsup> <mtext>CO</mtext> <mn>3</mn> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> <mo>-</mo> </mrow> </msubsup> </mrow> </math> is greater than that of <math> <mrow> <msubsup> <mtext>HCO</mtext> <mn>3</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> </mrow> </msubsup> </mrow> </math>.

The concentration of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mtext>H</mtext> <mo>+</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msubsup> <mtext>HCO</mtext> <mn>3</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> </mrow> </msubsup> </mrow> </math> are approximately equal.
questions.solution:
H 2 CO 3 ⇌ H + + HCO 3 - ; K 1 = 4.2 × 1 0 - 7 HCO 3 - ⇌ H + + CO 3 2 - ; K 2 = 4.8 × 1 0 - 1 1

∵   K 1 ≫ K 2 , so H 2 CO 3 ionises more than HCO 3 - and hence, contribution of H + is mostly due to ionization of carbonic acid. Thus, the concentrations of H + and HCO 3 - are approximately equal.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Calculate the molar solubility of calcium hydroxide $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2$ in $0.10 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ solution. The ionic product of calcium hydroxide is $5.5 \times 10^{-6}$.
questions.solution:
Given,
Ionic product of $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2=5.5 \times 10^{-6}$
Concentration of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution $=0.10 \mathrm{M}$
$\because$ Ionic product of $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2=5.5 \times 10^{-6}$
or, $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2=\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}\right]+2\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
Let, solubility $=S$
$\begin{gathered}\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\ S \quad(2 S)^2\end{gathered}$
Also, $K_{\mathrm{sp}}=4 S^3$
where, $K_{\mathrm{sp}}=$ solubility product
$\therefore$ Due to common ion effect,
Molar solubility of $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2=\frac{5.5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1 \times 0.1}$
$=5.5 \times 10^{-4}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The successive equilibrium constants for the stepwise dissociation of a tribasic acid are $K_1$, $K_2$ and $K_3$, respectively. The equilibrium constant for the overall dissociation is
questions.solution:
For a tribasic acid like $\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4$ has three ionisation constant. The overall ionisation constants $(K)$ will be product of ionisation constants of three steps, i.e. $$ K=K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3 $$ $\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-}$
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-}$
$\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}$
Adding Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get $$ \begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4 \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-} \\ K=\frac{\left[\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^3}{\left[\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4\right]} \end{gathered} $$ Multiply mathematical Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get $$ \begin{gathered} K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4\right]} \times \frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-}\right]} \\ \times \frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-}\right]} \\ K_1 K_2 K_3=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^3\left[\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4\right]} \end{gathered} $$ Overall ionisation constant $K, K=K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: $\mathrm{pH}$ of a buffer solution decreases by 0.02 units when $0.12 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid is added to $250 \mathrm{~mL}$ of a buffer solution of acetic acid and potassium acetate at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The buffer capacity of the solution is
questions.solution:
Buffer capacity, $\beta=\frac{d C_{\mathrm{HA}}}{d_{\mathrm{pH}}}$,
where, $d C_{\mathrm{HA}}=$ no. of moles of acid added per litre
$\begin{aligned}
d_{\mathrm{pH}} & =\text { change in } \mathrm{pH} \\
d C_{\mathrm{HA}} & =\frac{\text { moles of acetic acid }}{\text { volume }} \\
& =\frac{0.12 / 60}{250 / 1000}=\frac{1}{125} \\
\therefore \quad \beta & =\frac{1 / 125}{0.02}=\frac{1}{2.5}=0.4
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The minimum conc. of NH4Cl required to prevent the precipitation of Mg(OH)2 from a solution of 0.05 M Mg2+ and 0.05 M NH3 is x 10-3. What is the value of x in nearest whole number ?

(Ksp( Mg(OH)2) = 9.0 x 10-12 , Kb (NH4OH) = 1.8 x 10-5)
questions.solution:
Conc. of OH - = K sp Mg 2 + = 1.34 × 1 0 - 5

K NH 4 OH = NH 4 + 1.34 × 1 0 - 5 0.05

NH 4 + = 0.0671 M = 67.1 × 1 0 - 3 M

So, answer is 67.

Alternative solution

IF Mg(OH)2(s) is just formed then the equilibria existing are

Mg OH 2 s + Aq. ⇌ Mg 2 + + 2 OH aq - ; K sp 2 OH aq - + 2 NH 4 aq + ⇌ 2 NH 3 + 2 H 2 O ; 1 K b 2

Mg OH 2 s + 2 NH 4 aq + ⇌ Mg 2 + + 2 NH 3 + 2 H 2 O; ; K eq = K sp K b 2

K eq = 9 × 1 0 - 1 2 1.8 × 1 0 - 5 2 = Mg 2 + NH 3 2 NH 4 + 2

NH 4 + 2 = Mg 2 + NH 3 2 9 × 1 0 - 1 2 × 1.8 × 1 0 - 5 2

= 5 × 1 0 - 2 × 5 × 1 0 - 2 2 × 1.8 × 1 0 - 5 2 9 × 1 0 - 1 2

= 1 2 5 × 3.24 × 1 0 - 1 6 9 × 1 0 - 1 2 = 4 5 × 1 0 - 4

NH 4 + = 6.70 × 1 0 - 2 = 6 7 × 1 0 - 3

x = 6 7
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: $75 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}$ is mixed with $25 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $1 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{HCl}$. To this solution, $300 \mathrm{~mL}$ of distilled water is added. What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of the resultant solution?
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 & =75 \mathrm{~mL}, V_2=25 \mathrm{~mL} \\
M_1 & =0.2 \mathrm{M}, M_2=1 \mathrm{M} \\
V_3 & =300 \mathrm{~mL}, \mathrm{pH}=? \\
V & =V_1+V_2=75+25=100 \mathrm{~mL} \\
M & =\frac{V_1 M_1+V_2 M_2}{V_1+V_2}=\frac{75 \times 0.2 \times 25+1}{75+25}=0.4 \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Total volume
$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\prime}=V_1+V_2+V_3 & =75+25+300=400 \mathrm{~mL} \\
M V & =M^{\prime} V^{\prime} \\
0.4 \times 100 & =M^{\prime} \times 400 \\
M & =0.1 \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\text { Concentration of } \mathrm{HCl}=0.1=10^{-1}
$$
$$
\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=1
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A solution is prepared by mixing $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $1.0 \mathrm{M}$ acetic acid and $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.5 \mathrm{M}$ sodium acetate and diluted to $100 \mathrm{~mL}$. If the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ of acetic acid is 4.76 , then the $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{[\text { salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]} \\ & {[\text { Acid }]=\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}\right]=\frac{10 \times 1}{100}=0.1 \mathrm{M}} \\ & {[\text { Salt }]=\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]=\frac{20 \times 0.5}{100}=0.1 \mathrm{M}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{pH}=4.76+\log \frac{0.1}{0.1} \\ & =4.76\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: At \(T(\mathrm{~K})\), if the ionisation constant of ammonia in solution is \(2.5 \times 10^{-5}\), the \(\mathrm{pH}\) of \(0.01 \mathrm{M}\) ammonia solution and the ionisation constant of its conjugate acid respectively at that temperature are \((\log 2=0.30)\)
questions.solution:
\(K_a \times K_b=K_w=10^{-14}\) (at \(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\))
where, \(K_a=\) ionisation constant for acid \(\left(\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right)\)
and \(K_b=\) ionisation constant for base \(\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)\)
\(=2.5 \times 10^{-5}\)
i.e. \(\quad K_a\left(\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right) \cdot K_b\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)=10^{-14}\)
\(\begin{gathered}
\therefore \quad K_a\left(\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right)=\frac{10^{-14}}{K_a\left(\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right)} \\
K_a=\frac{10^{-14}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}=4 \times 10^{-10}
\end{gathered}\)
i.e. ionisation constant of conjugate acid \(=4 \times 10^{-8}\)
For \(\mathrm{pH}\) of \(0.01 \mathrm{M}\) ammonia \(\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}\) dissociate as :
\(\begin{array}{llll}
& \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \rightleftharpoons & \mathrm{NH}_4^{+} & +\mathrm{OH}^{-} \\
\text {At equilibrium } & 0.01 (1-\alpha) & 0. \alpha & 0.01 \alpha\end{array}\)
(\(\therefore\) Concentration of ammonia solution \(=0.01 \mathrm{M}\))
Also, \(\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_b}{C}}=\sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01}}=0.05\)
and \(\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\mathrm{C} \alpha=(0.01) \alpha\) or \(\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=0.0005\)
\(\therefore \quad \mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]\)
or, \(\mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14+\log [(0.01) \alpha]=14-3.3\)
Thus, \(\mathrm{pH}=10.7\) and also ionisation constant \(\left(K_a\right)=4 \times 10^{-10}\)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $10 \mathrm{~L}$ of a buffer solution containing $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ and $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ having $\mathrm{p} K_b$ of 5 ?
questions.solution:
Given, concentration of salt $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}\right)=0.01 \mathrm{M}$
Concentration of base $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}\right)=0.1 \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{p} K_b=5$
A mixture of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ forms basic buffer solution. The Henderson's equation for a basic buffer solution is
$\mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{p} K_b+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Base }]}$
$\mathrm{p} K_b=-\log K_b=-\log 0.1=1$
By substituting values in the above equation, we get
$\mathrm{pOH}=5+\log \frac{0.01}{0.1}=4$
$\mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14-4=10$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The solubility products of $\mathrm{NiS}, \mathrm{ZnS}, \mathrm{CdS}$ and $\mathrm{HgS}$ are $4.7 \times 10^{-5}, 1.6 \times 10^{-24}, 8 \times 10^{-27}$ and $4 \times 10^{-53}$ respectively. An aqueous solution contains $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}, \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}$ of equal concentration. $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ gas was passed into this solution very slowly. The first and the last ions that precipitate as sulphides are respectively.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Calculate the molar ratio of a weak acid \(\mathrm{H} A\left(K_a=10^{-6}\right)\) and its salt with strong base, so that the \(\mathrm{pH}\) of buffer solution is 6 .
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]} \log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]} \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{pH}}{\mathrm{p} K_a}=\frac{6}{6}=1 \\
& {\left[K_a=10^{-6} \therefore \mathrm{p} K_a=-\log K_a=\log \left(10^{-6}\right) \Rightarrow 6\right]} \\
& \log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}=1 \\
& \frac{[\text { Acid }]}{[\text { Salt }]}=10^{-1} \text { or } 0.1
\end{aligned}\)
Hence, the correct option is (d).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: $75 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}$ is mixed with $25 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $1 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{HCl}$. To this solution, $300 \mathrm{~mL}$ of distilled water is added. What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of the resultant solution?
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 & =75 \mathrm{~mL}, V_2=25 \mathrm{~mL} \\
M_1 & =0.2 \mathrm{M}, M_2=1 \mathrm{M} \\
V_3 & =300 \mathrm{~mL}, \mathrm{pH}=? \\
V & =V_1+V_2=75+25=100 \mathrm{~mL} \\
M & =\frac{V_1 M_1+V_2 M_2}{V_1+V_2}=\frac{75 \times 0.2 \times 25+1}{75+25}=0.4 \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Total volume
$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\prime}=V_1+V_2+V_3 & =75+25+300=400 \mathrm{~mL} \\
M V & =M^{\prime} V^{\prime} \\
0.4 \times 100 & =M^{\prime} \times 400 \\
M & =0.1 \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\text { Concentration of } \mathrm{HCl}=0.1=10^{-1}
$$
$$
\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=1
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the ionic product of $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_2$ is $1.9 \times 10^{-15}$, the molar solubility of $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_2$ in $1.0 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{NaOH} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\mathrm{CM}}{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}+\underset{\mathrm{CM}}{\mathrm{OH}^{-}} \\ & \mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_2 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ni}_x^{2+}+\underset{x}{2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}} \\ & \therefore \quad K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^2 \\ & =x(x+C)^2 \\ & \end{aligned}$
$$
=x\left(x^2+2 C x+C^2\right)
$$
or
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\mathrm{sp}} & \left.=x C^2 \text { (neglecting higher powers of } x\right) \\
x & =\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\mathrm{c}^2}=\frac{1.9 \times 10^{-15}}{(1)^2} \\
& =1.9 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: An aqueous solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>gram</mi></math> oxalic acid dihydrate is made up to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math>. The volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> required to completely neutralize <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>milliLiter</mi></math> of this solution is:
questions.solution:
We know that the molecular formula of oxalic acid dihydrate is (COOH)2.2H2O.

As the n-factor of oxalic acid is 2, the total equivalents of oxalic acid in 250 ml=2(6.3126)=110

Therefore, the total equivalents of oxalic acid dihydrate in 10 ml=110×1250×10

As 0.1 N of NaOH, having volume V is required to neutralize 1250 equivalents of oxalic acid dihydrate, so,

(0.1)V=1250⇒V=125 l =1250×1000 ml⇒V=40 ml
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Determine the solubility of silver chromate at 298 K given its K<sub>sp</sub> value is 1.1 × 10<sup>-12</sup>.
questions.solution:
Ag 2 CrO 4 ⇌ 2Ag + + CrO 4 2 - ; K sp = 1.1 × 10 - 12
   S                     2S            S

K sp = Ag + 2 · CrO 4 2 -

Ksp = [2S]2 · [S] = 4S3

S3= Ksp4 = 1.1×10-124

⟹S=6.53×10-5
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which one of the following species acts as both Bronsted acid and base?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{2-}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
$\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{2}^{-}$ is a conjugate base of $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{2}$ (a
monobasic acid) and does not give $\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{HPO}_{3}^{2-}$ is a conjugate base of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{3}^{-}$ and does not ionise further, since $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}$ is a dibasic acid.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The number of hydroxyl ions in $10 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of $0.2 \mathrm{M}$ HCl solution is
questions.solution:
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=0.2 \mathrm{M}$ (given, $\mathrm{HCl}=0.2 \mathrm{M}$ )
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-14}$
$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\frac{10^{-14}}{0.2}=5 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{M}$
Concentration of hydroxyl ions in $0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}$
$$
=5 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{M}
$$
$\therefore$ The number of moles of hydroxyl ions
$$
=5 \times 10^{-14} \times 1000
$$
The number of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ in $10 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{5 \times 10^{-14} \times 1000 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}{10} \\
=3 \times 10^{12}
\end{array}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The solubility product of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ is $32 \times 10^{-12}$. What is the concentration of $\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}$ ions in that
solution?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-} \\ & 2 s \\ K_{\mathrm{sp}} &=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}\right] \\ &=(2 s)^{2}(s) \\ K_{\mathrm{sp}} &=4 s^{3} \\ s &=\left(\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{4}\right)^{1 / 3} \\ &=\left(\frac{32 \times 10^{-12}}{4}\right)^{1 / 3} \\ &=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.005 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ solution?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-} \\ & \begin{array}{lll}0.005 \mathrm{M} & 0.005 \mathrm{M} & 0.005 \mathrm{M}\end{array} \\ & \mathrm{pOH}=-\log _{10}[\mathrm{OH}] \\ & =-\log _{10}[0.005] \\ & =-\log _{10}\left(5 \times 10^{-3}\right) \\ & =-\left[\log 10^{-3}+\log 5\right] \\ & =-[-3+0.699] \\ & =-[-2.301] \\ & =2.301 \\ & \mathrm{pOH}+\mathrm{pH}=14 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=14-2.301 \\ & =11.699 \\ & \approx 11.7 \\ & \end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a correct mathematical equation for Ostwald dilution law?
questions.solution:
Ostwald dilution law of weak electrolyte,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{HA}_{(\mathrm{aq})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{C} \quad-\quad- \\
& \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C} \propto \quad \mathrm{C} \propto \quad \mathrm{C} \propto \\
& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]} \\
& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{C} \propto^2}{1-\propto c}(1-\propto \simeq 1) \\
& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{C} \propto^2 \\
& \propto^2=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{C}} \\
& \propto=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{C}}}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The solubility product of $\mathrm{PbCl}_2$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ is $3.2 \times 10^{-5}$. What is its solubility in mol dm${ }^{-3}$ ?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}_{\text {sp }}=3.2 \times 10^{-5} \\ & \mathrm{PbCl}_{2(\mathrm{~s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Pb}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=4 \mathrm{~S}^3 \\ & \therefore \quad 4 \mathrm{~S}^3=3.2 \times 10^{-5} \\ & \mathrm{~S}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-5}}{4}} \\ & =\sqrt[3]{8 \times 10^{-6}} \\ & =2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \\ & \end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: A weak monobasic acid is $3.0 \%$ dissociated in it's $0.04 \mathrm{M}$ solution. What is the dissociation constant of acid?

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
MgOH2 is precipitated when NaOH is added to a solution of Mg2+. If the final concentration of Mg2+ is 10-10 M, the concentration of OH-M in the solution is

[Solubility product for MgOH2=5.6×10-12]
questions.solution:
In the saturated solution of sparingly soluble electrolyte, the ionic product of ions is constant at constant temperature and it is known a Solubility Product.

Here,

Mg(OH)2→Mg2+OH-2

Given,

Ksp for Mg(OH)2=5.6×10-12

Mg2+=10-10 M

Substituting the value, we get :

Ksp=Mg2+OH-2⇒5.6×10-12=10-10OH-2⇒OH-=0.24 M

Hence, the concentration of [OH-]=0.24 M
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Addition of sodium hydroxide solution to a weak acid (HA) results in a buffer of pH 6. If ionization constant of HA is <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the ratio of salt to acid concentration in the buffer solution will be:
questions.solution:
pH=pka+log  Salt Acid  

⇒6=5+log⁡  SaltAcid

⇒1=log ⁡SaltAcid

⇒log10=log ⁡SaltAcid 

Saltacid=101
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of monoacidic weak base is 10.9. Calculate the percent dissociation in $0.02 \mathrm{M}$ solution.
questions.solution:
Monoacidic weak base $\mathrm{BOH}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{BOH}_{(\mathrm{aq})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{C} \quad \mathrm{C} \alpha \quad \mathrm{C} \alpha \\
& \mathrm{C} \alpha \quad- \\
& \mathrm{pH}=10.9 \\
& \mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH}=14 \\
& \mathrm{pOH}=14-10.9=3.1 \\
& \mathrm{pOH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\
& {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-\mathrm{pOH}}} \\
& =10^{-3.1}=10^{-4}+0.9 \\
& =10^{-4} \times 10^{0.9} \\
& {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=7.9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}} \\
& {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\mathrm{C}_\alpha} \\
& \alpha=\frac{7.9 \times 10^{-4}}{0.02} \\
& \alpha=3.95 \times 10^{-2} \\
& \% \alpha=3.95 \times 10^{-2} \times 100 \\
& =3.95 \%
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.q: The <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mrow></msub></math> for <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> at <math><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo></math> C ( <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is a monovalent ion) is 3.2 <math><mo>×</mo><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The maximum concentration of <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> that could be attained in a saturated solution of this solid at <math><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo></math> C is
questions.solution:
X2SO4⇌2X++SO42-
Ksp=(2S)2×S=4S3
4S3=3.2×10-5
or S3=8×10-6
S=2×10-2 .

x + =2S
=2×2× 10 −2 =4× 10 −2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Acetic acid and propionic acid have <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></msub></math> values <math><mn>1.75</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math><mn>1.3</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively at a certain temperature. An equimolar solution of a mixture of the two acids is partially neutralised by NaOH. How is the ratio of the contents of acetate and propionate ions related to the <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></msub></math> values and the molarity?
options: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1.75</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>1.3</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>×</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">β</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">β</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> , whereas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">β</mi></math> are ionized fractions of the acids
The ratio is unrelated to the <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></msub></math> values
The ratio is unrelated to the molarity
The ratio is unrelated to the pH of the solution
questions.solution:
In a given mixture, the ionization of two acids can be written at
Let α,β be degree of ionization at same concentration.
CH3COOH1-α⇌CH3COO-α+H+α+β
C2H5COOH1-β⇌C2H5COO-β+H+α+β
So K Acetic acid  =  α α + β ⋅c 1−α
K Prepionic acid  =  β α + β ⋅c 1−β
So K acetic acid K propinoic acid  =  α 1−α  ×  1−β β
or α1-α=1.751.3×β1-β.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
How much of 0.3 M NH4OH should be mixed with 30 mL of 0.2 M solution of NH4Cl to give buffer solution of pH 8.65?
(Given: pK b of NH 3 =4.75 )
questions.solution:
It is a basic buffer
pH=8.65 ,
pOH = 14 – 8.65 = 5.35
pOH=pKb+log[N⊕H4][NH4OH]
5.35 = 4.75 + log [N⊕H4][NH4OH]
[N⊕H4][NH4OH]= antilog (5.35 – 4.75)
= antilog (0.6) ≈4.0
So [N⊕H4][NH4OH]=30×0.20.3×V≈4.0
So V = 5 mL.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: <math><mn>0.005</mn></math> mol of <math> <mrow> <mtext>Ba</mtext><msub> <mrow> <mfenced> <mrow> <mtext>OH</mtext></mrow> </mfenced></mrow> <mtext>2</mtext> </msub> <mtext> </mtext></mrow> </math> is dissolved in <math> <mrow> <mtext>100 mL</mtext></mrow> </math> of water. Assuming complete ionisation of <math> <mrow> <mtext>Ba</mtext><msub> <mrow> <mfenced> <mrow> <mtext>OH</mtext></mrow> </mfenced></mrow> <mtext>2</mtext> </msub> </mrow> </math> the <math> <mrow> <mtext>pOH</mtext></mrow> </math> of the solution will be
questions.solution:
100 mL of solution contain 0.005 moles of Ba OH 2  

∴ 1 L  1000 mL of solution contain

= 10 × 0.005 mole of Ba OH 2

Concentration of Ba OH 2 (i.e. moles on litres)

=  0.05 M

Each Ba OH 2 give 2OH − ions

Thus, moles of OH − per L =2×0.05=0.1

iii  ∵pOH = −log OH −

pOH = −log 0.1  = − log10 −1

pOH = log 10 = 1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The solubility of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> with solubility product <math><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math><mn>0.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> solution would be
questions.solution:
∴KspAgCl=SS+0.1

∵S≪0.1

∴S+0.1 ≈0.1

∴1.6×10-10=S×0.1

∴S=1.6×10-9 M

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The heats of neutralization of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>HCOOH</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>HCN</mi><mo> </mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kcal</mi></math> per equivalent respectively. The correct increasing order of acid strength is
questions.solution:
ΔHionisation=ΔHneutralisationexperimental-ΔHneutraltheoretical

ΔHionisationCH3COOH=-13.20+13.75=0.55 kcal mol-1

ΔHionisationHCOOH=-13.40+13.75=0.35 kcal mol-1

ΔHionisationHCN=-2.90+13.75=10.85 kcal mol-1

ΔHionisationH2S=-3.80+13.75=9.95 kcal mol-1

-13.75 kcal mol-1 is the standard enthalpy of neutralisation for strong acid or a strong base. Higher the ionisation enthalpy, weaker is the acid.


HCN<H2S<CH3COOH<HCOOH
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
H2S is bubbled through 0.2M NaCN solution which is 0.02M in each AgCN2(aq)-1 and CdCN4(aq)2-.  Determine which sulphide will precipitate first.

K sp Ag 2 S = 1 0 - 5 0 ,  K sp CdS = 7 × 1 0 - 2 8

K f  Ag  CN 2 - 1 = 2 . 5 × 1 0 2 0 ,  K f  Cd CN 4 aq 2 - = 1 0 1 7 .

S2- ion concentration for Ag2S and CdS may be reported as:
questions.solution:
2AgCN2aq-1⇌2Agaq++4CNaq-1;1Kf2AgCN2-12Ag++Saq2-⇌Ag2Ss;1KspAg2S
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2 Ag CN 2 aq - 1 + S aq 2 - ⇌ Ag 2 S s + 4 CN aq - 1 ; 1 K sp Ag 2 s K f 2 Ag CN 2 - 1 = K 1


K 1 = 1 0 + 5 0 6 . 2 5 × 1 0 4 0 = 1 . 6 × 1 0 9       K 1 = 1 K sp Ag 2 S ·  K f 2  Ag CN 2 - 1

1 . 6 × 1 0 9 = CN - 1 4 Ag CN 2 - 1 2 S 2 - = 0 . 2 4 0 . 0 2 2  S 2 -


∴    S 2 - = 2 . 5 × 1 0 - 9 M   for precipitation of Ag2S.


Cd CN 4 aq - 2 ⇌ Cd 2 + + 4 CN aq - 1 ; 1 K f Cd CN 4 2 - Cd aq 2 + + S aq 2 - ⇌ Cd S S ; 1 K sp Cd S
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Cd  CN 4 aq 2 - + S aq 2 - ⇌ 4 CN aq - 1 + Cd S s ;    K 2 = 1 K sp CdS ·   K f Cd CN 4 2 -


K 2 = 1 7 × 1 0 - 2 8 × 1 1 0 1 7 = 1 . 4 2 8 × 1 0 1 0


1 . 4 2 8 × 1 0 1 0 = CN - 1 4 Cd CN 4 2 - S 2 - = 0 . 2 4 0 . 0 2 S 2 -


S 2 - = 5 . 6 × 1 0 - 1 2   required for precipitation of CdS.

Lower sulphide ion concentration needed for CdS, so, it will precipitate out first.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A certain weak acid has a dissociation constant of  <math> <mrow> <mtext>1.0</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math>  . The equilibrium constant for its reaction with a strong base is :
questions.solution:
HX ⇌ H + + X -

K a = 1 0 - 4 = H + X - HX      .....(i)

HX + NaOH ⇌ NaX + H 2 O

K eq = Na + X - HX Na + OH - = X - HX OH -      .....(ii)

By Eqns. (i) and (ii)

K eq K a = 1 K w   ∴   K eq = K a K w = 1 0 - 4 1 0 - 1 4 = 1 0 1 0
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: An indicator has a pK<sub>a</sub> of 7.5. What is the ratio of the concentration of the base form to the concentration of the acid form ([A<sup>-</sup> ]/ [HA], in a buffer solution in which the pH is 6.5 ?
questions.solution:
Indicator is weak organic acid HA

pH = pKa + log A - HA

6.5 = 7.5 + log A - HA

log  A - HA  = - 1

∴   A - HA  =  0.10
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MY</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math>, two nearly insoluble salts, have the same <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mrow></msub></math> of <math><mn>6.2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>13</mn></mrow></msup></math> at room temperature. Which of the following statement is true with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MY</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math>?
options: The molar solubilities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MY</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> in water are identical.

The molar solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MY</mi></math> in water is less than that of the molar solubility of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> .

The salts <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MY</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> are soluble more in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>KY</mi></math> than the pure water.

The addition of salt <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>KY</mi></math> to the solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MY</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> does not have any effect on their solubilities.
questions.solution:
For MY, an insoluble salt,

Ksp=s2

MY s⇌M+ aq+Y- aq

For NY3, an insoluble salt,

Ksp=4s3

NY3 s⇌N+ aq+3Y- aq

∴ SMY=6.2×10-13=7.8×10-7

∴ SNY3=6.2×10-13413=5.2×10-5

∴ SMY<SNY3
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A solution is 0.1M in Cl- and 0.001M in CrO42-.
Solid AgNO3 is gradually added to it Assuming that the addition does not change in volume and Ksp(AgCl)=1.7×10-10M2 and
KspAg2CrO4=1.9×10-12M3.

Select correct statement from the following:
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> precipitates first because its <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub></math> is high.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ag</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CrO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> precipitates first as its <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub></math> is low.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ag</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CrO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> precipitates first because the amount of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ag</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> needed is low.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> will precipitate first as the amount of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ag</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> needed to precipitate is low.
questions.solution:
i Ag+ required to ppt AgCls

Ksp=IP=Ag+Cl-=1.7×10-10

Ag+=1.7×10-9

ii Ag+ required to ppt Ag2CrO4s

Ksp=IP=[Ag+]2CrO4-2=1.9×10-12

Ag+=4.3×10-5

Ag+ required to ppt AgCl is low so AgCl will ppt 1st.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math>-dinitrophenol (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pKa</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math>) acts as buffer and if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of this buffer is adjusted to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>, the ratio of concentration of dissociated and undissociated molecules of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math>-dinitrophenol is:
questions.solution:
pH = pK a + log C. Base acid (C.Base=Conjugate base)

5 = 4 + log C. Base acid

log C. Base acid = 1

∴   C. Base acid = Dissociated ions undissociated acid = 1 0
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Which of the following equations give ionic product of water?

(i) NH3aq+H2O⇆NH4aq++OHaq-

(ii) NH4aq++H2O⇆H3O++NH3aq

(iii) NH2aq-+H2O⇆NH3aq+OH-

(iv) NH3aq+OHaq-⇆NH2aq-+H2O
questions.solution:
The equation of the ionic product of water can be written as:

H2O⇌H++OH-or, 2H2O⇌H3O++OH-

The above equation can be obtained by adding the first two given equations:

NH3aq+H2O⇆NH4aq++OHaq-NH4aq++H2O⇆H3O++NH3aq2H2O⇌H3O++OH-

Hence, on doing basic manipulations in i & ii we are able to get required equation for ionic product of water
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
